content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I *NEED* a psychological mind bending movie with massive twist

**Score:** 134
**Body:** So I have started watching movies for a while and i realised that I have more intereste in Psychological mind bending movies here are my few top of the watchlist movies 

Inside man

Gone girl 

Coherence 

Old boy

Memories of murder 

The man from earth ( one of the best movie according to me just sitting there and destroying beliefs😅)

Gisaengchung aka parasite 

Se7en

Memento

Any recommendations based on this?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Identity
2. Incendies
3. The Others!
  - My older sis spoiled the ending of The 6th Sense before I was able to watch it, so I spoiled The Others for her. Yes, I chose violence that day. 
4. Triangle 
5. The Game
  - "You still in that contest thing?"
6. It's really more of a B thriller, but you might like Identity, with John Cusack. It's interesting, because a lot of the stuff that bugs you about it have a whole different meaning later. (It has a really good commentary track too.)
7. Triangle (2009)

The box (2009)

Arq (2016)

Timecrimes (2007)

Eraserhead (1977)

Pi (1998)

Primer (2004)

Coherence (2013)

Inland Empire (2006)

Lost highway (1997)

Videodrome (1983)

Existenz (1999)

12 monkeys (1995)

And really most films by David Lynch and David Cronenberg fit this billing


And for some none horror/thriller ones

Synecdoche new York (2008)

Sorry to bother you (2018)

Being John malkovich (1999)

Eternal sunshine of the spotless mind (2004)

The fountain (2006)

The fall (2006)

Adaptation (2002)




8. Falling Down starring Michael Douglas and Robert Duvall. It's best just watching without knowing anything about it. Just superb.

## 2. What’s a disturbing horror movie that genuinely made you feel uncomfortable and uneasy the entire time?

**Score:** 68
**Body:** Not just jump scares or gore, but that constant disturbing atmosphere where something feels deeply wrong from beginning to end. The kind of movie that sticks in your head after watching and makes you feel weird for hours or even days.

Looking for films that feel psychologically unsettling, disturbing, surreal, hopeless, or just deeply uncomfortable to sit through.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I wasn’t a huge fan, but Martyrs(the original, not the us one) was pretty unsettling. 
2. We need to talk about Kevin

Irreversible
3. Midsommar
4. Others with Kidman. It’s more thriller than horror but that feeling that something is very wrong is so powerful. It’s a stunning movie too 
5. The Ugly Stepsister 
6. It Follows. Maybe, even a decade later, the uneasiness from it has never left me.
7. The Shining does this so well for me I get a little nauseous from it.
8. The Good Son
9. Hereditary for sure
10. Antichrist 

## 3. Suggest the Most mind bending movie of all time.

**Score:** 36
**Body:** Smtg that makes me wanna re-watch the movie or think abt it for days coming and still not understand completely. I've watched inception and shutter Island. Thanks

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Mulholland Dr
  - Seconding Mulholland Drive and adding Momento
2. The Holy Mountain

Good luck figuring out that one. :)  

Warning:  It will put bizarre images of things in your mind that you will never be able to forget or unsee and that you will think about years later.
3. Coherence

4. Nobody suggested "Eraserhead"?!  Really?!  Or did I miss it?
5. Beyond the Black Rainbow

Enter the void

Infinity Pool
6. Primer (2004)
  - Then his next movie Upstream Colour, even more of a mindfuck.
7. The Prestige 
The Fountain 
8. Arrival (2016) - concept is very interesting and thought-provoking. 

Annihilation (2018) - one of my all-time favorites, won’t spoil. 

## 4. 'The Conversation [1974] Gene Hackman, Harrison Ford, Robert Duvall

**Score:** 35
**Body:** After all these years, I finally got around to this one, and wow - it's really good. Francis Ford Coppola creates a tense film-noir about a really repressed guy who does surveillance for a living, and gets too involved with a certain conversation.

It has so many things I like. The 70s fashion and cars, shot in San Francisco, and the truly geeky level of 'gear' - surveillance / sound equipment - lots of time shifting and 'impressionistic' moments, with a music concrete type soundtrack.

Gene Hackman is gripping as Harry Caul [modeled after Harry in 'Steppenwolf'] - giving perhaps an even better performance than as Buck in 'Bonnie and Clyde.'

Harrison Ford is really the most 'distinct' character in the movie, being very terse and businesslike, mysterious and threatening too.

Teri Garr has some scenes, and John Cazale [Dog Day Afternoon, The Deer Hunter, The Godfather] is Harry's assistant. Several other characters gather for a party in Harry's workshop and these scenes are not to miss. Robert Duvall [uncredited] has a couple of muffled lines, it's strange.

It's totally coincidental that the movie was released just a few months before Nixon resigned because of the Watergate Wiretapping Scandal - it wasn't planned that way. Neither was the fact that the same state-of-the-art equipment used in the movie was used in the Watergate Hotel.

This goes on my re-watch list. I'm actually late to the party; there are trailers and a bunch of commentaries and DVD special bits on youtube - so it's been high on the list a long time.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I agree it is really excellent.  Now you have to watch Enemy of the State  (Will Smith), to find out what happened to Harry.   
  - Ok so is he revising his charcter from the concersation in enemy of the state? I just assumed he became a jazz musician. 
2. So, where was the bug? ......Or, was there a bug?
3. Brilliant movie, beautifully paced. 
  - Yes, that struck me from the start - it was 'stealthy,' the pacing.
    - It created an organic tension, didn't rely on soundtrack or artificially imposed techniques, and that's difficult to achieve. 

Hackman and Cazale just kill it from start to finish, too.
4. The twist at the end is one of the finest ever.

It’s the one film John Cazale starred in where he’s just fine but not the Cazale many rave about.
5. Masterpiece 
6. I thought Cindy Williams did a great job too.  Between this and American Graffiti I wish she'd been given more opportunities to work outside of the confines of Laverne & Shirley.
7. The main theme is possibly my favourite score ever. I don’t play the piano but I had to learn it after seeing the movie, it was haunting me.

## 5. Normal (2026)

**Score:** 28
**Body:** How did this movie bomb so hard?

Saul Goodman(Bob O) goes to a small town in Minnesota called Normal, population around 1,800, as a temporary sheriff, and slowly gets embroiled in a complete shitstorm.

First half is a slow burn, but the movie is solid. The second half has some really cool action scenes, and honestly, I enjoyed this more than Nobody 2.

We need more movies like this once in a while just to change the flavor. Not every action movie needs to be nonstop explosions or fast paced with hyper cuts from beginning.

This one really did not deserve to bomb that badly.

Give it a shot.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I was thinking about it the other day and wondering when it was going to be released.

I guess there's your answer: No advertising.
2. Movies usually bomb because of poor marketing and limited releases. Movies also bomb if they aren’t already well known IP.
3. Personally with Normal coming out so soon after the release of Nobody 2 and them both featuring the same leading actor and action premise I’m not in any particular hurry to watch it. 
4. My family was super excited to see it and had no idea they missed the theatrical release, it was only out for like two weeks apparently. 
5. I like the actor in small increments. There’s just something about the tone of his voice that irritates me. Same with Nick Kroll.
6. Nobody 2 was awful. Shame cause the first was great and I love the director 
7. Never heard of it. Looking it up now, its not showing in 3 local cinemas and the only one it *is* showing in is screening it only once a day and too late for me.
8. I never even heard of it somehow, definitely will give it a go 
9. It was okay bit of a letdown but fun non the less.
10. It’s a fucking Ben Wheatley film starring Bob Odenkirk and I only knew it existed because I follow the director on Instagram. It’s like someone’s actively paying to suppress it, not just that they’re not advertising it.

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Which moment in a movie or show nailed you to the ground ? The epilogue of HBO show Chernobyl .

**Score:** 360

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Band of Brothers. The interviews with the surviving men after each episode tore me up 
  - Well if you’re already in the shredder.. If you really wanna humanize them try out Winters’ book of ww2 letters, Hang Tough. This thread is a great.  

https://www.reddit.com/r/BandofBrothers/comments/1anpnj6/surprising\_and\_interesting\_finds\_in\_dick\_winters/

”June/July 1945: “When it comes to combat, I go along beautifully. Can understand things and movements, and plans just come naturally. In garrison, I like the soldiering part, but as for social activities, I am a Class A flop.”
  - It was the “Why We Fight” episode for me.  Crushed me every time.
2. series is fantastic a must watch but nothing hit me as hard as the firefighter episode. They seem them fine in the beginning but then remember what Legasov said about radiation poisoning and then proper wtf, just so sad
  - Watching the mini series provided me with a healthy fear of acute radiation poisoning. All of your DNA ripped to shreds by neutrons, then slowly and painfully disintegrating into goo while all your body’s cells die. 
  - It's so horrifying because it's so silent. There are some mild symptoms of being hit with massive radiation (tasting metal appears in the show, for example). But unless you're being hit with "dead in 30 minutes" radiation, you don't know there's anything wrong. Arguably, "dead in two weeks" radiation is far more horrifying than "standing over the core and getting hit with the equivalent of a nuclear bomb" radiation. At least the latter is over pretty quickly.

If you pick up something hot... you drop it. It hurts. If you touch something electrically energized, you recoil away from it (hopefully). But pick up a chunk of graphite that's extremely irradiated? Absolutely nothing. Nothing meaningful, anyway. Until later you realize that your hand is basically dead tissue rotting on your body.

For me it was the helicopter scene. Just the helplessness of those on the ground. It was sort of a micro image of the macro reality. Knowing what was coming, watching it happen, and being able to do nothing. Once they were in that cone of danger of immense radiation, their radios were dead. Along with any sensitive electronics in the helicopter like some instruments. But the truth is even if it hadn't crashed; none of them were going to survive anyway. 

Radiation is genuinely scary af.
3. The whole series is execllent and of my favourite documentary mini series. I have watched it multiple times already.

America currently reminds me of how the soviets were portrayed in this show.

"Our true power comes from the perception of our power"
  - It's not a documentary. 
4. Thing is, he knew the exposure would be lethal. Went anyway.

Hero. The whole lot who did their duty.
5. There's a film called The Captain, about a psycho who deserts from the Wehrmacht and finds an Officer's coat. He puts it on and pretends to be a Captain, dupes a bunch of soldiers to follow him and basically goes on a murderous hedonistic rampage, corrupted by the power his new uniform grants him.  
  
About halfway through the film it suddenly drops that this bonkers, terrifying tale is a true story. Knocked me sideways.

## 2. My Dad’s favorite film

**Score:** 335
**Body:** Romancing the Stone (1984) staring Michael Douglas, Kathleen Turner and Danny Devito.   I saw Jewel of the Nile (1985) first then this second, opposite of my father. This one was always his favorite.  I secretly liked Jewel of the Nile a little more because I thought we got more Danny Devito in it when I was little but he is in both of them. Have you seen these two films?  Which one is your favorite?  Highly recommend them if you haven’t seen them.  

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Loved Romancing the Stone.

THE Joan Wilder?!?
  - I read your books!
    - "They told you I had a car? They are such comedians. They meant my little mule: Pepe!"

My dad had an old beat up pickup truck we called Pepe because of this movie.
2. '84 Kathleen Turner was throwing HEAT
  - So gorgeous! 
3. Love this movie!!!!!  I made my family watch it a few weeks ago.  Douglas, Turner and Devito are such a great trio.   Romancing the stone is the better of the two IMO. 
4. Watched it a while ago. There are few scenes as memorable as his jungle mud slide into her lap. Great film.
5. He has excellent taste!
6. Rarely hear anything about these movies, both are some of my mom's favorite movies, and one of those movies that if it was on TV we would always watch it. 
7. Love that movie and much better than the sequel. 

## 3. I don’t have a lot of faith in sequels in today’s era of movies but this one definitely deserves another go

**Score:** 172
**Body:** We get “fast and furious 18” and “Jumanji 6”…but we can’t get another one of these…I know horror/ fantasy isn’t as profitable as “blockbuster action” films but even films like “Insidious” got other spinoffs galore…maybe I’m in the minority on this but I really enjoyed this film and it feels like a missed opportunity 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Loved this movie.
2. A bloated, uninspiring mess.
But enough about me, Doctor Sleep was good fun. 
  - You had me in the first half, not gonna lie
3. It's a shame but at least we got rose the hat!

![gif](giphy|Ke2EAK3zHu3rLltGQ1)
4. Re-watched it 2 nights ago. 10/10
5. I've only seen the directors cut and loved it. I have no idea how you remove 30 minutes without hurting the film however. 
  - The theatrical cut omits the chapter breaks and a bunch of backstory / development for just about every character. And you are correct, removing this hurts the film. You might argue that it’s paced better, but this isn’t a film that really benefits from tighter pacing, spending more time in this world with its lore and characters makes for a better movie.
6. I liked Doctor Sleep alright, but generally speaking, I'd argue that the best horror movies (or series) work better as contained stories.

Most attempts at monetizing on the success of a good horror movie through sequels will inevitably lead to the thing that made them scary in the first place being over-explained or getting an unnecessary backstory, until it loses all its mystique and becomes either tedious or ridiculous.  
Good horror almost always works because you can never fully grasp the motivation of the scary thing that lurks in the dark, because if you could understand it, it just wouldn't be as scary. The less you know about it, the more you feel a certain tension. You will ask yourself questions that have no clear answers, so your mind fills in the gaps, often relying on your personal fears to some extent, which is much more engaging than having everything spelled out for you. Most of what writers would come up with to rationalize an entity would never live up to the primal fear it instills in you through more abstract means anyhow.

Doctor Sleep feels more like an adventure movie than a pure horror movie, and as someone who has read the Dark Tower books, I can enjoy it as such. I'd compare it to how Aliens worked as a sequel to Alien because it was an Action movie instead of trying to be a horror movie and having to recreate the atmosphere and suspense that made the first one work so well.

Also, low to mid budget horror movies are actually quite profitable, that's why Blumhouse will keep shitting them out until they stop making money.
7. Great film
8. I like it better than the Shining. 

## 4. Would you agree with Terrence Fletcher’s way of teaching?

**Score:** 143
**Body:** *Whiplash (2014)*

At the end it is proven to be effective. Although it is undoubtedly abusing and violent. Would you agree with his method “there are no words in the English language more harmful than «good job»”? 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|daPCSjwus6UR2JxRX1)
2. He had a student kill himself. Did we watch the same movie?
  - ![gif](giphy|JEVRfQblU1XbjaYf7g)
3. As someone who has coached a lot of individual athletes. There is some merit to his approach but the abuse is completely unnecessary. You can extract greatness with a firm tough approach but he goes beyond that. Fear isn’t necessary but respect is. 
  - Got that Joe Jackson vibe of beating greatness out of young people 
    - And if the beatings dont work you're not beating them frequent enough or with the required power to produce the desired result.
4. No
5. >At the end it is proven to be effective.

Even if it’s effective, at what cost? Lifelong trauma?

Don't think any skill is worth that much, the same goes for parental disciplining. There are effective ways to teach and learn, this is not one of them.
6. Jesus, no. I thought I was in r/moviescirclejerk for a minute there.
7. ![gif](giphy|pD7YIQoUwgb9cnX3FJ)

  - But in Iceland

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. The Banshees of Inisherin made me realize we are all inside the comedy

**Score:** 100
**Body:** After watching The Banshees of Inisherin, I kept wondering which of the two was actually right.

At some point, a quote by Natsume Sōseki came to mind:  
“Tragedy is greater than comedy.”

People only straighten their posture when faced with death.  
Only then can they become truly serious.

Rejecting someone, cutting off your own fingers and throwing them at the other person, even the audience trying to analyze which side is right — all of it felt like nothing more than people performing a comedy.

What happens in this film is something completely ordinary for human beings.

The reason it feels insane is because we ourselves are living inside the comedy.

That’s why I thought it was such an incredible film.

In the end, the two men descend into attempted murder and both survive.  
And I felt that, only then, for the first time, they were finally able to truly face each other.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I had so much sympathy for Padraic for most of the movie but the young lad was correct when he said (probably bastardising the quote) " I used to think you were the best of them but now I see you're just like the rest of them".


He's not a good person, but he's invested in the idea that people think he is, and and he's trying to force something similar on the people around him who love him (live in this small box of others' expectations with me). Both his sister and Colm have bigger ambitions and only become happy / realise them by leaving his presence. 


He's a tragic character because he cannot see the prison he's created for himself and the ones he loves.
2. Tragic film, truly. Padric and Colm both were simultaneously in the wrong and in the right. Padric didn't respect Colm's boundaries, but he was right to be upset about an incredibly sudden shift in their relationship which left him without a friend.

Colm was an asshole about the situation and to Padric, but he tried time and time again to get Padric to leave him alone which led to disfigurement, a dead pet, and a burned down house. 

The further Colm withdrew the further Padric pursued. It was incredibly tragic and the film has stuck with me.
  - The disfigurement was a personal, insane choice.
    - That struck me as a metaphor about the Irish Civil War, which is obliquely part of the film.
3. Is life better spent crafting a legacy by making a piece of art that people will remember, or by going down to the pub being nice and having a drink?

Both characters are of course wrong and unhealthy in the way they engage in life, yet their conflict hits on something true. Is life for enjoyment and being kind, or for leaving something behind?
4. Kubrick said that comedy is more realistic than tragedy, because it takes into account the absurd


Also neither man was "right" - Colin WAS a shiftless loser, that's why his wife left him, but Brendan was blaming Colin for his own lack of artistic development which was irrational, there was no actual need to be so cold to him in order to work on his music



  - You might be thinking of his sister who he lives with, who leaves during the movie? I don't recall it being said that he was ever married. I think his sister really loved him, but she was unhappy with the plainness of their isolated village life on the island.

## 2. Art is subjective but Mandy is generally a horrifying film.

**Score:** 68
**Body:** Man, so many positive things I could say about this film. The acting. The visual display that is in front of you. The colours. The way Linus shows you his complete naked body to the viewer. . 
Let me catch my breath. I love this film. Cage is an exciting actor. Anything he is in you are enticed because Cage.

The moment when the camera fades to Jeremiah and Mandy’s face. Melts like volcano lava into the picture.it’s really peak 

The movie switches from a drug induced romance, to horror to straight up what the fuck am I watching. Peak cinema, the kind that makes you feel emotion.

My favorite moment is the importance of the number 44. When you notice you realize.

It’s a romantic horror film. The best kind. Some people might say it’s slow but okay art is subjective. But man the awesomness of Mandy is real.

Also you can buy the 44 shirt and tiger shirt. I own both

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It’s an incredible work of visual art and such a fun, wild ride. I love the cheekiness of the dude pulling out a longer chainsaw and it just keeps coming. The 80s metal meets 60s psychedelia influences. The horn of abraxos. I mean, cmon. Legendary. 
2. mandy is a great one time viewing experience, especially in a theatre. i failed to recapture that vibe on second or third screenings, but nevertheless that first time viewing was legendary for me. 
  - Couldn’t agree more. That red glow was just everywhere and the first 30 minutes are truly haunting. But once you know it goes off the rails, it’s hard to rewatch with the same dread.
3. I just love the insane display of obscure references in it. From the Helter Skelter-like sect to the heavy metal subculture. The color palette is nothing but extraordinary, like some mesmerizing merging of biometrics and psychoanalysis. Insane.

There some 'romantic horror,' too, but *Mandy*'s overarching concept is that of a revenge tragedy, conflating with various cosmic/urban horror themes.  Let it suffice to say that those into Dennis Cooper literature will feel quite at home while watching *Mandy*.

I think it's not that far-fetched to posit that the experience of watching this movie for the first time is up there with *Blade Runner* and *Terminator* *II*.
4. Favorite point, minus all the awesome bits everybody listed? The meltdown over the vodka midway through.



Easily one of the greatest moments of anger in film. Not a single bit of dialogue said. It's hilarious, and then, you're like "where are we goin' with this." In classic fashion, the answer is "it's goin' down."

  - I agree so fucking much with what you wrote.

Edit: yeah man definitely
    - Apparently Cosmatos' Flesh Of The Gods and Nekrocosm are both already in production or pre production. So hopefully, we get more nightmares from 1983.

      - I know, thank you for the endorphin hit. He’s my kinda director
5. Modern classic. The shots, the dread, especially the score. 

johan johansson is one of the greatest to ever do it and his other work is worth checking out. Adds so much. Sicario is another one of my favorites with his stuff in it. Really wish he could've done more metally stuff like Mandy. RIP

Honestly though I don't love Nick Cage in this, and normally I do, but Mandy feels like he's operating at 80%. Every other member of the cast outshines him, particularly the cult guy and Mandy too. I'm not well versed so maybe a director thing. A better actor or Cage firing on all cylinders and Mandy would be one of the greatest of all time, and it still is in spite of him
6. It made my top 5 of the past decade. And yes, it is absolutely case evidence that art is subjective. Some movies I go out of my way to make a case for, others, well, it's in the eye of the beholder, and I don't have any feelings one way or another if anyone says they don't like it. God knows it's not a perfect film, is filled with flaws, as far as parts of the acting, script, pacing... it has such a particular and peculiar aim... but it's so absolutely unique, and it takes me to that transported place outside of thought and analysis in a way that only my favorite films do. I don't know how much I care to interpret it. I don't even know what 44 means. But it provided me that temporary release from the bondage of self, and for that I'm grateful.

## 3. Do different film genres require different ways of evaluation?

**Score:** 13
**Body:** Hi everyone,



I’m currently working on a personalized movie rating app, and I’m rethinking how films should be evaluated depending on their genre.



The question I’m exploring is whether different genres should be judged with different priorities.



For example, a horror film might rely more heavily on atmosphere, tension, sound design, or imagery, while a documentary might depend more on perspective, clarity, access, impact, or the way it frames its subject. A comedy might be judged more on rhythm, timing, character dynamics, or rewatchability.



Of course, every film still needs fundamentals like writing, direction, sound, and visual language. But I’m interested in whether some criteria should matter more or less depending on the type of film.



If you have a genre you know particularly well, I’d be curious to hear:



1. What do you think matters most when evaluating that genre?

2. What does that genre need to do differently from others?

3. Are there criteria that are often overrated or underrated for that genre?

4. Can a film in that genre be great even if it fails at something usually considered important?

5. What examples best represent your view?



I’m not trying to define a universal system. I’m more interested in understanding how people who know a genre deeply actually evaluate it.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This methodology in general goes against the way I personally think of film criticism, even extending to rating, much more so than framing the issue as one of genre.

In my experience, different criteria are going to matter more or less on a completely individual, film-by-film basis. A story that has excellent characterization and flimsy plot can be great when the director knows how to frame that story around the characters. A film that is all plot with barebones characterization can also work just as well, in a context where the intricacy of plot is framed as priority. And a film that does both well might be better or worse than either of these depending on any number of other criteria.

I can’t speak to anyone else’s methodology, but I don’t view film criticism as a checklist of elements that each add up to a score. When I grade a film, I’m grading the experience that I have, and if a film can achieve that experience in completely unexpected ways, not aligning with any of my expected or preconceived notions of *how* it accomplishes that, I sure don’t want weighted math determining the degree of enthusiasm I attach to that experience.
  - There's a relatively well-known film critic, Mike D'Angelo, who rates everything on a 100 scale. And you know, I get it that film critics who actually publish professionally often have to provide a "consumer reports" rating of stars, or a grade. But every time I read D'Angelo I'm like, what's really the difference between a 68/100, or a 69/100?
  - I have a friend who is consistently ranking movies and updating his "top 25" and, to each his own, but I just don't view movies that way. There are definitely movies I like better than others, and movies that I would argue are of a higher quality than others, but if I'm comparing two movies that I both enjoy and think are high quality, I have a very hard time deciding which one is "better," even subjectively.
2. I feel like a lot of you folks are far too data driven. It's like you want to check boxes and add up values to figure out if you should like something or not.

Watch a movie and feel the way you feel about the experience FIRST. Then try to understand what it was that made you feel the way you felt about it. Don't turn it into some qualification contest.
  - That’s fair, and I actually agree.

The feeling should always come first. I don’t want the app to tell people whether they should like a movie or not.

For me, it’s more about watching the film, feeling the experience, and then understanding why it worked or didn’t work.

I’m also deep into data through my studies and my job as a web analyst, so this project is a way to connect my love for films, how real movie fans think, and my own data/product skills.

So yeah, not replacing emotion with numbers just trying to better understand the emotion afterwards.
3. >The question I’m exploring is whether different genres should be judged with different priorities.

Not a fan of this for multiple reasons:

* genres are incredibly fluid
* films almost always sit in between genres to some extent, and I'd say *most*
* it feels like a very, I don't know, high-school way of looking at film
4. By definition, such a way of judging films would only really work for strict genre films. Anything that falls in between those boxes (which are probably most films discussed in this subreddit, for example) would be impossible to rate like that. You need to rate them in a film by film basis, and for that you need to understand the authorial intent first and foremost. And authorial intent is not restricted by genre (unless that's what the authors actually intend, in which case we are again talking about strict genre films, or a very specific selection of films).
5. There are genre conventions and types that usually come into play that sometimes requires consideration whenever evaluating. 

Mortality and ability changes whether action movie or horror as an example. 
6. I'm not sure that there are genre-specific criteria so much as a 'does the film do what it set out to do' criteria. You take a film on it's own merits. Sleepaway Camp isn't Sophie's Choice. Ferris Bueller's Day Off isn't Martyrs. Resident Evil isn't Dude, Where's My Car. Rather than having separate classifications for reviews, you need to make it very clear that the movie is being taken as a whole film that realizes its vision. Are the characters consistent? Does the plot move in a clear and logical way? Do the scenes segue into each other smoothly? Is the shot framing legible in a way that maintains story integrity? Is the theme inherent in both the beginning and the ending? Whether you're talking about Flash Gordon or Schindler's List, a lot of these questions are going to be universal.

## 4. The Tragedy of Frank’s Parasocial Fantasy in The Bride!

**Score:** 3
**Body:** Frank has no healthy blueprint for love or partnership, so he studies romance through a movie star he watches on a screen, a man who is physically “off” (one leg shorter than the other) and yet framed as desirable and adored. That’s the closest he ever gets to seeing a body as “wrong” as his welcomed instead of rejected.

What devastates me is that Ronnie only ever exists for Frank as a fantasy. In Frank’s head, Ronnie is proof that a broken body can be loved. In reality, when they finally meet, Ronnie treats him like dirt: laughs at him, talks down to him, recoils as if Frank is too filthy and deformed to be allowed in the same space.    
You can see Frank go through the five stages of grief in seconds lol 😂 . He could have easily pulverized Ronnie’s head the way he crushed those men’s skull outside the club, but he doesn’t. Instead, he dances. He retreats into the only language they “share”: the choreography and elegance he memorized by watching Ronnie on screen.

Seen that way, his lie to the Bride makes more sense, even if it’s \*\*still inexcusable\*\*. He’s not drawing from any lived experience of reciprocal love. The best “relationship scripts” he has are all about control, staging, and keeping the other person trapped inside his fantasy. Lying becomes a way to hold onto the movie in his head. what makes it so brutal when he finally owns the lie, calls himself a “black hole,” a “monster,” and the Bride answers, “so am I”, two people admitting they’re dangerous to others as well as to each other.

That’s very close to what Mary Shelley does with the creature in the original novel. The creature is right that Victor Frankenstein wronged him, created him, abandoned him, denied him any model of kindness or belonging. But in the end, he tearfully admits that this doesn’t excuse the innocent people he killed or the way he wasted his own free will on revenge. He owns the fact that his pain is real and that the way he responded to it made everything worse.    
Frank feels like a modern version of that. He is treated monstrously, and he really has been given almost no healthy tools for love. But the movie still shows that the patterns he clings to(idealizing Ronnie, lying to the Bride) are his, and they hurt the one person he actually doesn’t want to “obliterate.” That’s what makes his late self awareness so devastating (him owning the lie and saying “I am a black hole, a monster”): by the time he starts to own his monstrosity and loosen his grip, it’s already too late. He’s destroyed.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think he's a fascinating artist. Much like Tarkovsky, Lopushansky also grasped the aesthetic influences as a moral guidance. What he did with *Dead Man's Letters* is just stunning, a unique interpretation of spiritual malaise within a sci-fi, dystopian landscape. 

Although I can't concur on *Visitor to a Museum* being 'the best Russian movie ever made,' his overall work in it is nothing but great. Lopushansky has been in the business for a long time, so he has a first-person perspective regarding both technicalities and political obstacles regarding art in modern Russia.

I'd like to recommend his interviews, as he have shared important information about his own lifework, Tarkovsky, and several other takes on Russian and European cinema in general.
